title: AI Tools Every Small Business Needs

modules.title: Why AI Is Now Affordable for Small Business
modules.sub: Understanding how AI costs dropped within reach of entrepreneurs
modules.description: Learners explore the economic shifts that have made AI tools accessible to businesses without large budgets or technical staff. They assess common misconceptions about AI complexity and cost, building confidence to evaluate tools realistically. By the end, they can articulate a clear business case for why AI investment makes sense at their scale.

modules.title: Automating Customer Service With AI
modules.sub: Deploying chatbots and AI assistants to handle customer interactions
modules.description: Learners evaluate and configure AI-powered customer service tools such as chatbots and automated ticketing systems suited to small business operations. They work through real scenarios from retail and hospitality to decide which interactions to automate versus keep human. The module concludes with a plan for launching a basic AI customer service touchpoint in their own business.

modules.title: AI-Powered Marketing on a Budget
modules.sub: Creating smarter campaigns using affordable AI content and analytics tools
modules.description: Learners use AI writing, image, and analytics tools to build marketing assets without agency-level spend. They compare free and low-cost platforms and practice generating copy, social posts, and email campaigns tailored to their audience. The module challenges them to design a lean AI-assisted marketing workflow they can repeat consistently.

modules.title: Smarter Inventory and Supply Chain Decisions
modules.sub: Using AI forecasting to reduce waste and prevent stockouts
modules.description: Learners examine how AI-driven demand forecasting and inventory tools work for small-scale product businesses. They apply decision frameworks to sample data sets to practice identifying overstock risks and supply gaps before they become costly. The module asks them to map at least one AI-assisted intervention point in their own inventory or ordering process.

modules.title: Using AI for Financial Planning
modules.sub: Leveraging AI tools to forecast cash flow and control costs
modules.description: Learners explore AI-enabled accounting, cash flow forecasting, and expense-tracking tools designed for small business budgets. They work through guided scenarios to interpret AI-generated financial projections and identify where automated alerts could prevent common cash flow crises. By the module's end, they select and justify a financial AI tool appropriate for their business stage.

modules.title: Hiring and HR: AI That Helps Fairly
modules.sub: Applying AI to recruiting and HR while managing bias risks
modules.description: Learners examine how AI screening, scheduling, and onboarding tools can save time in small business hiring workflows. They critically assess bias risks embedded in AI hiring tools and practice applying fairness checkpoints before deploying any system. The module requires them to draft a short responsible-use policy for AI in their own hiring process.

modules.title: Choosing and Evaluating AI Vendors
modules.sub: Building a rigorous framework to vet AI tools and providers
modules.description: Learners develop a structured vendor evaluation checklist covering pricing models, data privacy terms, integration requirements, and support quality. They apply the checklist to real vendor examples, practicing the skill of reading contracts and privacy policies critically as a non-technical buyer. The module produces a reusable scoring rubric they can apply to any future AI purchasing decision.

modules.title: Building an AI Adoption Roadmap
modules.sub: Sequencing AI investments into a practical, phased business plan
modules.description: Learners synthesize insights from all previous modules to construct a personalized AI adoption roadmap for their business. They prioritize tools by impact and implementation effort, set realistic timelines, and identify internal champions or skill gaps that need addressing. The final deliverable is a one-page action plan ready to share with partners, staff, or investors.

synopsis: Small business owners do not need a tech team to start benefiting from AI — they just need the right roadmap. This hands-on course walks entrepreneurs through the most practical, affordable AI tools available today, with real examples from retail, hospitality, services, and more.
rationale: A demand score of 5/10 alongside a corpus similarity of 0.61 against a narrow existing module on budget forecasting signals that small business owners need a comprehensive, role-specific course rather than isolated financial tools instruction.
